faq:
Frequently Asked Questions

Are ducks and geese more at risk for bird flu than chickens?

Yes. Waterfowl owners face higher risk because the virus spreads through wild bird droppings and shared water, and Santa Cruz County sits along the Pacific Flyway migration route. Keeping ponds and water sources inside enclosed areas helps lower that exposure.

What should I do if I find sudden deaths or sick birds in my flock?

Watch for warning signs like sudden deaths, a drop in egg production, nasal discharge, swollen or purple combs and wattles, and severe lethargy. If you suspect avian influenza, call the CDFA hotline at 866-922-2473, where testing for suspected cases is free.

Is bird flu dangerous to my family?

The human health risk remains low, and there has been no sustained person-to-person spread. Still, it is sensible to use basic biosecurity like boot covers and handwashing after working in the run.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I use compost as mulch, or mulch as compost?

Compost can work as a surface mulch when spread on top of soil, but mulch cannot replace compost as a soil amendment. Fresh wood chips and other raw mulch materials pull nitrogen from the soil as they break down, so they should never be mixed into vegetable beds.

How much compost and mulch should I apply, and how often?

A good rhythm is to work about one inch of finished compost into empty beds once a year at planting time, then layer two to three inches of mulch on top. The mulch protects the soil and slowly feeds it as it decomposes over the months.

Why is mulch especially helpful in Santa Cruz gardens?

Mulch retains soil moisture and suppresses weeds, which is particularly valuable during our dry summers when water conservation matters. Adding mulch wherever bare soil shows between plants keeps roots cooler and cuts down on watering.

Frequently Asked Questions

How can I tell when the run needs a full cleaning?

Do a simple smell test: if you can detect ammonia, the cleaning is overdue. Most flocks do well with a complete bedding change every one to three months, plus weekly spot removal of wet or soiled bedding around water sources.

Which bedding material lasts the longest?

Wood shavings absorb moisture well and last longer than straw, which compresses and turns soggy quickly. Dried leaves are a free option but break down faster, so they need replacing more often.

Can I put the cleaned-out bedding straight onto my garden?

Not right away. Fresh poultry manure is too high in nitrogen and can carry pathogens, so let it age and compost for about three to six months first. Once composted, it becomes an excellent garden amendment.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I plant tomatoes outside in March in Santa Cruz?

Coastal gardeners can risk setting out tomatoes and peppers after mid-March if they use row covers for protection. Inland growers should wait until April, when the soil has warmed and frost risk has fully passed.

What can I direct-sow in March?

Beans, carrots, beets, and lettuce are ready for direct seeding, and coastal gardeners can also sow cilantro, dill, and arugula. Summer-blooming bulbs like dahlias and gladiolus, plus sunflower seeds, can go directly in the ground too.

How do I handle the pests that show up in early spring?

Watch for earwigs and cutworms as conditions warm. Trap earwigs with rolled newspaper or oil-filled containers, and protect young transplants from cutworms with simple cardboard collars around the stems.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do scare tactics like reflective tape and fake owls actually work?

Not reliably in Santa Cruz County. Local birds quickly learn that these objects are harmless, so they offer only short-lived protection. Lightweight bird netting with three-quarter-inch mesh, draped as fruit ripens and secured against gaps, is far more dependable.

Why are squirrels harder to keep out than birds?

Squirrels chew through standard netting and can leap up to eight feet horizontally from fences, roofs, or nearby branches. The most effective defense is physical: hardware cloth cages around fruit clusters, sheet metal baffles on trunks, and pruning branches back at least eight feet from any launching point.

When should I net my fruit trees?

Net about two weeks before the fruit ripens. In Santa Cruz County that usually means early to mid June for stone fruits and late summer for apples and pears.

Frequently Asked Questions

Do I need a big yard to plant a rainbow garden with my kids?

No. A rainbow garden works in any size space. If you do not have a large bed, you can use seven individual containers on a patio or balcony, one for each color of the rainbow.

Which plants work for the harder colors like blue and indigo?

For blue, try cornflowers, borage, or lobelia, and for indigo reach for larkspur or purple beans. These are chosen to bloom reliably in coastal and central California's USDA Zones 9 and 10.

What makes a rainbow garden good for learning?

Arranging plants by color gives children a clear, hands-on way to observe how different plants grow. Garden-based learning has been shown by UC Cooperative Extension to strengthen science skills and encourage time outdoors.

Frequently Asked Questions

At what temperature do my birds start to struggle with heat?

Poultry begin experiencing heat stress above 85 to 90 degrees, and temperatures over 100 degrees can be fatal, especially for heavier chicken breeds. Since birds cannot sweat, they cool themselves by panting and holding their wings away from their bodies.

What is the fastest way to cool my flock on a hot day?

Provide ample shade with 70 to 80 percent shade cloth over the run during peak afternoon hours, and offer multiple water stations, since chickens can drink up to twice their normal amount in the heat. Adding ice blocks to waterers and shallow wading pools for ducks and geese helps further.

Should I change what I feed my flock during a heat wave?

Yes. Skip scratch grains and corn during hot spells, since digesting them generates internal body heat. Offer hydrating treats like watermelon and frozen fruit instead.

Frequently Asked Questions

Which crops are best for succession planting?

Fast-growing vegetables like lettuce, radishes, arugula, cilantro, beans, and beets work especially well. Radishes mature in just 25 to 30 days, so you can fit several plantings into a single season.

How far apart should I space my plantings?

A simple and reliable interval is two to three weeks. Sow a short row, wait two to three weeks, then sow again, so that by the time you finish harvesting the first row the next is ready.

Does Santa Cruz's climate help with succession planting?

Yes. Our long growing season lets you sow lettuce from February through November, and coastal microclimates stay mild enough to stretch cool-season crops even further. Keeping a simple calendar of planting dates makes it easy to stay on track.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is bolting cilantro a sign I did something wrong?

No. Cilantro is genetically programmed to flower and set seed once daytime highs regularly pass 75 degrees and days grow longer. It prefers cooler conditions between 50 and 70 degrees, so warm weather, not a mistake, is the cause.

How do I keep cilantro coming all season?

Succession planting is the most effective approach: sow small patches every two to three weeks from March through May and again from September through November. Slow-bolt varieties like Calypso or Leisure can add a couple of extra weeks per planting.

Should I pull cilantro as soon as it bolts?

Not necessarily. The flowers attract beneficial insects, and if you let the plant finish, the seeds become coriander you can use in the kitchen. Afternoon shade and consistently moist soil can also slow bolting in the meantime.

Frequently Asked Questions

When should I spray to prevent peach leaf curl?

Apply a copper-based fungicide during dormancy, either in late November to December after leaves drop, or in late January to early February before buds swell. Warmer areas like the Pajaro Valley and Scotts Valley should treat by mid-January, while cooler, foggier zones may have until late February.

What if my tree's buds are already showing green?

Once green or pink appears at the bud tips, spraying will not help that season, and copper can damage emerging foliage. Plan to time your application next dormant season before any bud swell begins.

Are there peach varieties that resist leaf curl?

Yes. Resistant varieties like Frost, Indian Free, and Q-1-8 tolerate the disease better, though no peach is completely immune. Choosing one of these can reduce how often you need to spray.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can a goose actually protect my chickens from predators?

Think of a goose as the alarm, not the lock. Its loud calls and territorial behavior give early warning and may intimidate small threats like stray cats or hawks, but it cannot fight off raccoons, foxes, coyotes, or bobcats.

Is it okay to keep just one goose?

Geese are highly social, so keeping a single goose raises welfare concerns, and pairs or small groups are generally recommended. A solo goose can adapt in a mixed flock, especially if it was raised alongside other birds from a young age.

What should I rely on instead for real protection?

Physical security comes first: hardware cloth enclosures, predator-proof latches, and buried wire around the perimeter. Only after those are in place should a goose be added as an extra layer of alertness.

Frequently Asked Questions

Should I start tomatoes and peppers in February?

Yes, start them indoors in early February. Good tomato choices include Early Girl, Sun Gold, and Cherokee Purple, and peppers germinate best with bottom heat around 80 degrees.

What can I plant outside in February?

Coastal and valley gardeners can direct-sow peas, radishes, and seed potatoes, and transplant broccoli, cabbage, and cauliflower under row cover. Mountain gardeners should wait, since frost risk lingers four to six weeks longer at higher elevations.

What maintenance should I finish before spring?

Complete dormant pruning of fruit trees and roses by mid-month and apply a second dormant spray if needed. It is also a good time to remove winter weeds before they set seed, work in about two inches of compost, and check your irrigation system.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is January too early to start tomatoes and peppers indoors?

For coastal California, yes. Starting these warm-season crops in January leaves them leggy and rootbound before it is warm enough to transplant. Aim for mid-to-late February instead, counting back six to eight weeks from your transplant date.

What can I start from seed in January?

January is ideal for cool-season crops like broccoli, cauliflower, cabbage, kale, and lettuce, which need only four to six weeks indoors before transplanting. Onions also work now, though they need a longer eight to ten weeks inside.

Will later-started warm-season plants fall behind?

No. Tomatoes and peppers started at the right time catch up quickly once they go into warm soil, often outperforming plants that were rushed too early.

Frequently Asked Questions

Will planting citronella or lavender keep mosquitoes out of my yard?

Not on their own. While plants like citronella grass, lemon eucalyptus, lavender, rosemary, and basil contain repellent compounds, UC IPM notes that living plants release far less oil than needed to create a real barrier. The oils only release when the leaves are crushed or brushed.

What actually reduces mosquitoes in a garden?

The most effective step is eliminating standing water where mosquitoes breed. Check pot saucers, gutters, and birdbaths, refresh birdbath water every two to three days, and treat rain barrels or ponds with mosquito dunks containing Bti.

Are there natural predators that help with mosquitoes?

Yes. Encouraging dragonflies and bats can help keep populations down. They work best alongside removing breeding sites rather than as a standalone solution.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I put compostable or biodegradable bags in my organics cart?

No. The rule is no plastic in the organics bin, and that includes bags labeled compostable or biodegradable, since facilities cannot reliably process them. Place food scraps and yard waste in loose, without plastic liners.

Do I keep food scraps and yard waste together or separate?

It depends on where you live. City of Santa Cruz residents must keep food scraps and yard waste separate due to landfill permit rules, while County and GreenWaste service areas like Live Oak, Soquel, Aptos, Capitola, and Scotts Valley combine them in one green cart.

Can the curbside organics cart take things my home compost pile cannot?

Yes. Curbside bins accept cooked food, meat, dairy, bones, and large volumes of yard waste, which home piles cannot safely handle. Keep home composting to plant-based scraps, coffee grounds, eggshells, and brown materials to avoid attracting rodents.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why do my blueberry leaves turn yellow with green veins?

That pattern points to iron chlorosis caused by soil pH that is too high. Blueberries need acidic soil between 4.5 and 5.5, but Santa Cruz County soil often runs 6.0 to 7.0, which locks up iron so the roots cannot absorb it.

How do I lower my soil pH for blueberries?

Test the soil with an inexpensive pH probe or kit, then work in soil sulfur according to the package directions. Mulching with acidic materials like pine needles or aged sawdust helps maintain the lower pH over time.

Could the yellowing be something other than pH?

Possibly. Overwatering can stress the roots, and a nitrogen deficiency shows up as an overall pale yellow-green color rather than yellow leaves with green veins. Growing in containers with pre-acidified azalea or rhododendron mix makes the conditions easier to manage.
